name: Master of Architecture and M.S. in Management

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The architecture half is exemplary and well-ordered: the full M.Arch spine appears (Studio I-IV plus Advanced Studios I-II at deepestPrereqChain 5, with History I-II, Construction I-II, Environmental Control Systems I-II, Structures I-II, and Professional Practice I-II timed to support the studios). However, this is a dual degree with an M.S. in Management, yet not a single management, finance, or operations course was parsed among the 21 referenced courses, so the integration that justifies the joint degree is entirely undocumented.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the management-side courses (organizational behavior, finance, operations, strategy) to the plan and show explicitly how the M.S. in Management sequence interleaves with the M.Arch studio spine.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy on the architecture side, reaching a sensible depth of 5 through the studio chain (501G to 506G) with clean technical sub-sequences (Structures I to II, History I to II). Only two dangling references exist (missingCourseCount 2: ARCH 507G and MARC 701, the latter likely the joint-degree capstone), a minor blemish.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the ARCH 507G and MARC 701 references by adding those course records, since MARC 701 appears to be the integrative capstone the dual degree hinges on.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data, which is a notable gap for a dual professional degree where the whole value proposition is a defined blend of design and management competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish joint learning outcomes that state what graduates can do at the architecture-management intersection (e.g., firm leadership, project financing, real-estate development) and map them to courses.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, creditReconcileDelta is null, and no requirement groups were parsed, so a prospective student cannot see the total load or how credits split between the M.Arch and M.S. Management halves. For a dual degree (which normally trims credit through shared electives) this credit opacity is a substantive transparency defect.
dimensions.suggestion: State the combined total credits and show the credit savings versus taking the two degrees separately, broken out by architecture core, management core, and shared credit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The page reads as an M.Arch listing with the management half missing entirely; there is no plan of study, no credit total, and no labeled terms, and one prerequisite field (ARCH 542G) has description prose bled into it. The architecture descriptions themselves are strong, which only sharpens the contrast with the absent management content.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the page as a genuine dual-degree layout: two labeled tracks, a combined plan of study, a credit summary, and the management course descriptions.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The architecture courses reflect solid contemporary emphases (sustainable/passive environmental systems in ARCH 543G-544G, structures, professional practice), and pairing design with management is itself market-relevant for practice leadership and development. But with no visible management, real-estate finance, or BIM/computational-delivery content, the distinctive currency of this joint degree is unproven in the data.
dimensions.suggestion: Foreground real-estate development finance, construction project management, and integrated project delivery (BIM/VDC) as the concrete bridge between the two degrees.

descriptionQuality.justification: The architecture course descriptions are substantive and specific (e.g., ECS on passive thermal/visual comfort, Structures on gravity and lateral behavior), clearly full entries rather than placeholders; the limitation is coverage, since no management course descriptions were captured at all.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Architecture prerequisites are stated as explicit course codes with sensible sequencing, but clarity is reduced by the two dangling references (ARCH 507G, MARC 701), by ARCH 542G's prerequisite string absorbing description text, and by the complete absence of management-course prerequisites.

missingTopics.rationale: A joint architecture-management degree should teach pro formas, capital stacks, and feasibility analysis; no FIN/real-estate course appears among the parsed courses despite the college offering ARCH 651 Public and Private Development elsewhere.

Professional Practice I-II cover legal/ethical roles, but there is no dedicated project-management, scheduling/cost-control, or design-firm operations course, which is exactly where the management degree should add value.
Beyond the introductory Tools and Techniques courses, no BIM-coordination or IPD course is present, yet digital project delivery is the primary arena where design and management now converge.
An M.S. in Management implies foundational business coursework, none of which is visible, leaving the management competency undefined on the page.

missingTopics.topic: Design research methods and joint capstone
missingTopics.rationale: The referenced MARC 701 hints at an integrative capstone, but no research-methods vehicle is documented to prepare students for an evidence-based joint thesis.

emergingTrends.rationale: Real-estate and design decisions are increasingly driven by market analytics and PropTech platforms; a management-architecture graduate should be fluent in these tools.

emergingTrends.trend: Embodied carbon and ESG in development
emergingTrends.rationale: Investors and clients now weigh life-cycle carbon and ESG metrics; the joint degree should connect sustainable design (already present in ECS) to financial and reporting frameworks.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins and lifecycle asset management
emergingTrends.rationale: Buildings are increasingly managed as digital twins across their lifecycle, a natural bridge between the design and management competencies this degree combines.
Generative design and AI-driven business analytics are entering both professions; the Tools and Techniques and management sequences should address them critically.

emergingTrends.trend: Integrated project delivery and lean construction
emergingTrends.rationale: IPD and lean methods reorganize how firms manage risk and collaboration, squarely at the design-management intersection this program targets.

topSuggestions: Add the M.S. in Management coursework (business core, finance, operations, strategy) to the page so the dual degree is more than an M.Arch listing.
Publish the combined total credits and a joint plan of study showing shared credit and the two tracks side by side.
Resolve the MARC 701 and ARCH 507G dangling references, clarifying MARC 701 as the integrative capstone.
Introduce or foreground real-estate development finance and construction/firm project management as the concrete bridge between design and management.
State joint learning outcomes describing what graduates can do at the architecture-management intersection.
